summary: Joel Spolsky curates positive feedback from prominent tech bloggers about Evidence-Based Scheduling (EBS), the flagship feature of FogBugz 6.0. The post collects endorsements from Jeff Atwood, Rafe Colburn, Scott Rosenberg, and Reg Braithwaite, each highlighting different aspects of the feature. The testimonials praise EBS for improving estimation accuracy, encouraging focus through time tracking, and being a fully shipped product rather than just a concept. Spolsky also links to his earlier article on EBS published in Better Software Magazine.
key_points: Jeff Atwood praised EBS as a crucial feature unmatched by other software lifecycle tools for producing good estimates
Rafe Colburn found the time tracking feature useful not just for estimation but as a tool for maintaining focus and avoiding multitasking
Reg Braithwaite emphasized the critical difference between having a prototype of an idea and actually shipping it as a polished product
The post serves as a curated collection of social proof from respected voices in the software development community
money_quotes: I'm not aware of any other software lifecycle tools that go to such great lengths to help you produce good estimates.
When you kick off the timer on a task, you don't want to jump around and multitask because it will just throw off the timer.
Prototypes are not shipping products. FogBugz is a shipping product. My prototype was not. And that makes all the difference.
key_insight: Evidence-Based Scheduling in FogBugz 6.0 earned praise from the developer community for turning estimation theory into a shipped, practical tool that also encouraged better work habits like sustained focus.
